stories.title: The Glassblower's Gambit
stories.text: In the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, where glass-makers had worked for three generations, Merin's hands moved through fire and sand with the precision of a surgeon. The Syndicate's new edict seized all ornamental glass for the war effort. Common glass became state property. The beautiful became forbidden. Merin had a secret: her grandmother had taught her to sing through glass, to set voices in the silicate matrix before the cooling. Three cups in her vault held the last songs of the old queens, unheard for decades. If she surrendered them, the empire would melt them down for weapon-glass, drowning history in steel. Instead, she smashed her furnace. In the ruins, she hid the three cups in the kiln's heart, then walked out and told the collectors the fire had taken everything. They beat her for carelessness. She let them. Merin found them and offered a bargain: carry the cups, one each, into the deep forests where the songkeepers still sang. Sing them back awake when you're safe. The wanderers—a carpenter, a weaver, and a bone-singer—accepted.

stories.title: The Harbormaster's Reflection
stories.text: Captain Vesta had kept the harbor of Verdant Strand safe for fifteen years through logistics, iron will, and a gift for reading the tide's moods. When the deepkin envoy arrived—pale-skinned, cold-eyed, bearing terms for the new covenant—Vesta knew something was wrong. The envoy's shadow fell wrong on the docks. It moved independently, a fraction of a heartbeat delayed. She didn't challenge him. Instead, she offered hospitality: rum, fresh catch, a night in the harbormaster's tower. As they drank, Vesta asked casual questions about the deepkin realms, the underwater cities, the taste of abyss-water. By dawn, Vesta understood: this was not the envoy. It was a reflection—an autonomous echo wearing borrowed flesh, trying to negotiate for something the real commander had decided was too dangerous to risk. The shadow had learned to move like a person, but not like *that* person. Vesta didn't kill him. Instead, she sailed him back to the boundary-waters herself, to the place where the abyss was visible from the surface.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Hunger Turns Inward
stories.text: Sethrak the Fragment-Hoarder had accumulated gold for two hundred years. It filled caverns. It spilled from his claws. It was *his*—the only certain thing in a world where flesh aged and oaths broke. Not gone. Worthless. The distinction was agony. Sethrak tried to hoard consciousness-fragments instead, thinking value had simply shifted. He hunted merchants, bone-singers, wanderers—anything containing enough awareness to trade. But fragments slipped through his claws like water. They didn't accumulate. They *dissolved* into something larger, something he couldn't hold or own. Standing in his cavern, surrounded by mountains of useless gold and slipping fragments, Sethrak realized the truth: he had never actually possessed anything. He had only rented space in a world that was slowly deciding to take it back. He did not rage. Instead, he began to eat the gold. Coin by coin, ingot by ingot. His throat burned with ancient hunger. His belly filled with inert metal. And with each swallow, he felt something unexpected: lighter. Emptier. Freer.

stories.title: The Bone-Carver's First Song
stories.text: Mael the bone-carver had learned her trade from the old masters, before the Ascension changed everything. She could shape bone to sing, to remember, to speak truths that soft flesh could not hold. When the Dominion's agents came to her workshop demanding she carve confession-bones for the accused, she refused. They threatened her. Threatened the children in the adjacent ward-school. But Mael understood something the agents did not: bone has its own will. It will not sing lies cleanly. The bones she carved shattered when they tried to force falsehoods through them. Her refusal cost her workshop, her reputation, everything—until the Counter-Archive found her in hiding. They asked her to carve differently: bones of exoneration, of hidden truth, of protection. She began again with a steady hand and a clear conscience. The bones sang true. By winter, the Dominion's confession-protocol had collapsed. Mael had learned her real trade at last: not to carve what was demanded, but to sing what the bone itself insisted was true.

stories.title: The Merchant's Last Gambit
stories.text: Hadrin had spent forty years in spice caravans, moving goods from the Scorched Reach to the coastal markets. He knew the prices of saffron, starflower, the rarer compounds. He knew which merchants cheated and which could be trusted. But he had never seen anything like the substance the fae-touched scout brought to his warehouse in Pelimor. It was seed-like, but luminous. It grew warm in the hand. Three seeds had crossed the veil. She would trade all three for safe passage west, to the coast, away from the Dominion's auditors. Hadrin should have refused. Fae-trade was restricted. Selling it meant his merchant's charter would be burned. But the scout was starving, and the seeds—the seeds called to him in a language that had nothing to do with coin. He hid the scout in his warehouse and arranged her passage on a merchant vessel bound for safer waters. In return, she left him the three seeds.

stories.title: The Tide-Caller's Bargain
stories.text:
Kess the Tidecaller had inherited her mother's task: to tend the Saltmere estuary and keep it alive. For forty years, she'd walked the docks at dawn, reading the water's moods, maintaining the delicate balance between tides and city. The Dominion's new Harbor-Master wanted to dam the estuary. Progress, he called it. Profit. Kess found the merfolk captain—Nerida, her mother's rival and ally—waiting in the flooded ruins beneath the city. "Your people destroy the waters," Nerida sang, her voice like drowning, like grace. "One man destroys it," Kess said. "Not me."

"Then stop him."

That night, Kess climbed the Harbor-Master's tower with evidence: three centuries of tide-charts showing how the estuary's cycles fed the watershed, protected the city from storm-surge, nourished the fisheries that kept Verdant Strand alive. She spread the maps across his desk and asked him to read, truly read, what his dam would erase. The Harbor-Master studied the patterns and understood. Some systems were not built for speed. Some were *built for survival*. He signed the charter protecting the estuary. The dam was never constructed.

stories.title: The Hoarder's Last Lesson
stories.text:
Sorrath the Fragment-Hoarder had lived for three hundred years in the Ironspine caves, watching gold become worthless. The Luminous Ascension had unmade value itself. Now consciousness-fragments mattered more than coin. "I need to know why you hoard," the girl said. "My sister was taken by the Tower's hunger. I need to understand what you're hunting."

Sorrath, ancient and tired, considered turning her away. Instead, he descended from his hoard-pile—gold and silver tumbling like sand—and sat at her eye level. "I hoard because hoarding *is*. Because accumulation shapes identity. Because letting go means dissolving." His voice was smoke and regret. "But the Ascension changed the rules. Now I hold nothing but habit."

"Then help me," she said. "Tell me how to break the Tower's hunger."

He laughed—a sound like rocks grinding. "You cannot break hunger. You can only *teach it* to eat itself."

The scholar stayed three nights. Sorrath gave her his last treasure: not gold, but memory.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Choice
stories.text: Naela the bone-carver had learned the Old Language from Kess, the bone-singer who taught mercy instead of resurrection. But mercy was difficult. Forgetting was easier. She took the carving-knife and began. But the fourth bone—a child's rib, small and fragile—would not sing true. When she carved the Old Language into it, the language itself *refused*, cracking under her stylus, the sound emerging wrong: not an accusation but a *mourning*. She understood then what Kess had taught: the old language could not lie without breaking. It was bound to truth in ways human speech was not. Naela smashed her workshop and walked into the Wailing Scar—that liminal place where reality flickered between versions. There, in the half-space, she found Kess waiting, and also found herself five years hence, teaching the Old Language in a hidden catacomb. She chose the second path. The Scar released her whole. Three times, she sang the refuse-song—the Old Language worked backward—and the auditors left with no memory of why they'd come. By winter, whispers spread: the bone-singers were making a stand.

stories.title: The Spice-Runner's Inheritance
stories.text: Kelvan the spice-merchant had inherited his route from his mother, but not her luck. The Scorched Reach that once yielded starflower and saffron now yielded only ash. The Ascension had rewritten what grew where. He was three weeks overdue reaching Crimport when he found the caravansary in ruins, bodies wrapped in silk—merchants from the Seventeen, all dead. Their cargo lay scattered: boxes of starlight turned to inert crystal, gold that was simply *gold* again. In the master's tent, a leather journal. It detailed a hidden passage through the Wailing Scar, a liminal path where the old spices still thrived, where value held meaning beyond the Dominion's ledgers. Kelvan could have reported it. The Dominion would pay well for such knowledge. Instead, he burned the journal and took one of the dead merchants' cloaks—rust-colored, marked with the symbol of the Seventeen. He walked into the Scar alone, leaving his caravan at the border. The path wasn't a place; it was a *decision*.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Gambit
stories.text:
Korra the glasswright knelt before the furnace in her Keshtir workshop, watching the molten core pulse like a heart. The Dominion's auditors had seized three of her competitors this month alone—ledger-cheaters, they called them, though everyone knew the real crime was refusing to sell their secrets to the crown. She had one advantage: her glass remembered. Each pane she forged carried a fragment of her will. When the Dominion's inspector arrived at dusk, his boots clicking on tile, Korra was ready. She offered him wine in a cup she'd made that morning—sapphire-blue, beautiful enough to break hearts. The glass whispered as he drank. Not poison. Something older. A single image: the inspector's own hands, twenty years ago, breaking his master's kiln in a theft that had never been solved. He went white. The cup fell and shattered into a thousand pieces that caught the lamplight like stars. "How?" he whispered. "Glass remembers who shaped it," Korra said quietly. "And sometimes, it remembers the hands that break it too."

The auditor left. Her workshop was never inspected again.
